slides.title: Authenticated ECH Config Distribution and Rotation
Framing PQ + T Signatures for TLS
Use of Composite ML-DSA in TLS 1.3

polls.text: Do you want the list to get more functional?
Should the chairs be more draconian in their dolling out of moderation actions?
Should the chairs be more draconian in their dolling out of moderation actions?
Do you think FATT procedural discussions are a distraction?
Do you think chairs should be the ones that determine whether a draft goes for the initial sniff test?ss
Do you think authors and those performing the formal analysis should collaborate?
Should the TLS WG adopt draft-sullivan-tls-signed-ech-updates?

chat.author: Deb Cooley
chat.text: always

chat.author: Nicola Tuveri

ERR_TOO_MANY_CERTIFICATES

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: A can be secure. B can be secure. A+B can be terribly insecure.

chat.text: Transcription said MLS certificates, but he meant MTCs, no?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Dennis Jackson said:

A can be secure. B can be secure. A+B can be terribly insecure.

Precisely

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Multiple certificates

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Felix Linker said:

Transcription said MLS certificates, but he meant MTCs, no?

No, I meant "supplemental certificates"

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: To be clear, I think it's great that we have formal review of EKU, and if we determine that we're not picking up supplemental certificates, then we don't need joint review

chat.author: Felix Linker
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

To be clear, I think it's great that we have formal review of EKU, and if we determine that we're not picking up supplemental certificates, then we don't need joint review

you're referring to this work? https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosomakho-tls-supplemental-auth/

chat.author: Yaroslav Rosomakho
chat.text: I think we should take this one step at a time and determine first if TLS working group have any appetite for a given item

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: The reason I'm phrasing it this way is that suppose we publish EKU and then discover that in order for it to work with supplemental certificates, we need to tweak EKU in some small way. We'll then have been very sorry that we published supplemental certificates. And given that they are the same proponents....

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Felix Linker said:

Eric Rescorla said:

To be clear, I think it's great that we have formal review of EKU, and if we determine that we're not picking up supplemental certificates, then we don't need joint review

you're referring to this work? https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosomakho-tls-supplemental-auth/

Precisely

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Yaroslav Rosomakho said:

I think we should take this one step at a time and determine first if TLS working group have any appetite for a given item

Agreed. I was trying to say that.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: draco, not vlad the impaler, it turns out

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: please do not impale people, I like the chairs and would prefer they stay out of jail. But like one peg below that seems about right.

chat.author: Nicola Tuveri
chat.text: From Latin Dracō (stem Dracōn-) + -ian,[1] from Ancient Greek Δράκων (Drákōn), after the Athenian lawmaker Draco, known for making harsh laws. See δράκων (drákōn, “dragon”).

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: To echo what I was saying earlier, I think the chairs need to feel empowered to structure the conversation. That's explicitly contemplated in RFC 2418

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: And push people to be respectful, adhere to the code of conduct

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: I agree that it's very important to have dissenting opinions. I believe those have been very loud and repetitive from some. Once or twice or thrice, fine.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Bas Westerbaan said:

I agree that it's very important to have dissenting opinions. I believe those have been very loud and repetitive from some. Once or twice or thrice, fine.

And this is precisely what 2418 contemplates.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Even the repetition is somewhat fine by me, but the litigating, relitigating, calling on chairs to do things, saying that the FATT should be doing things (which is up to the chairs), ....

chat.author: Flo D
chat.text: I'm not sure twice or thrice is fine if you're just saying the same thing.  Make your point and trust that the chairs will take it into account.  It's different if there's an actual technical debate going on and you're adding information.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: All of this depends on participants exercising restraint.  Keep on topic.  Don't respond to every email that takes a position in opposition to yours.  Make messages respectful.  Don't bring private exchanges to the list.  Back off when your message has been heard.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Rough consensus means all objections have been addressed, even if not resolved.

chat.author: John Gray
chat.text: +1 Martin

chat.author: Sophie Schmieg
chat.text: In the plenary it was called a DOS, which I agree with, and to defend against a DOS you need to drop packets

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Even the much lower bar of shutting down the personal attacks would make me happy.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Structuring the conversation to avoid re-litigation would be nice, but I imagine would be nearly a full-time job for the chairs for some recent issues, so can't really be the norm.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Dennis: that's some subfloor, not even the floor :sweat_smile:

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text:
Right. 
Thom Wiggers said:

Dennis: that's some subfloor, not even the floor :sweat_smile:

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: how low can it go....

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Dennis Jackson said:

Structuring the conversation to avoid re-litigation would be nice, but I imagine would be nearly a full-time job for the chairs for some recent issues, so can't really be the norm.

Well, I think that one of the benefits of moderation is to set a general set of norms with the hope that people will conform to them going forward without individualized moderation of each post

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: FATT is supposed to be a sanity check.  Answer a simple question: does this draft need more formal analysis?  That's all.  This doesn't need to be a whole big thing.

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: :wait_one_second:

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

FATT is supposed to be a sanity check.  Answer a simple question: does this draft need more formal analysis?  That's all.  This doesn't need to be a whole big thing.

The issue is that this becomes a blocker if the draft author doesn't have the necessary resources to analyse the protocol.

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: TLS 1.3 PSKs were indeed a massive misfire.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: This complaint about analysis/implementation balance isn't really relevant to a discussion about FATT.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Martin Thomson said:

FATT is supposed to be a sanity check.  Answer a simple question: does this draft need more formal analysis?  That's all.  This doesn't need to be a whole big thing.

The issue is that this becomes a blocker if the draft author doesn't have the necessary resources to analyse the protocol.

Feature, not a bug? If the WG feels analysis is necessary for standardization.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Dennis Jackson said:

Jonathan Hoyland said:

Martin Thomson said:

FATT is supposed to be a sanity check.  Answer a simple question: does this draft need more formal analysis?  That's all.  This doesn't need to be a whole big thing.

The issue is that this becomes a blocker if the draft author doesn't have the necessary resources to analyse the protocol.

Feature, not a bug? If the WG feels analysis is necessary for standardization.

Yes.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Yeah, I lack the skills necessary for formal analysis, but I still recognize the importance of having done that work.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: It's a barrier to me changing the key schedule or whatever, but that's probably for the best.

chat.author: Deirdre Connolly
chat.text: It's not (and should not be) solely on document authors to do or find people to do any formal analysis if the FATT thinks it would be a good idea

chat.author: Deirdre Connolly
chat.text: Part of the documented process including the involvement of the chairs is to match make

chat.author: Bob Beck
chat.text: I hope we get to see what Thom is printing...

chat.author: Nicola Tuveri
chat.text: the 3d printer is very distracting Thom :slight_smile:

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Deirdre Connolly said:

It's not (and should not be) solely on document authors to do or find people to do any formal analysis if the FATT thinks it would be a good idea

Of course. The draft is the work of the working group. The "author" or "editor" just holds the pen. If the WG is counting on the author to do all the work, it's not much of a working group.

chat.author: Deirdre Connolly

chat.author: Thom Wiggers

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: So the draft I proposed ages ago gave a place for people to extend the protocol that was guaranteed to be "no weaker than unextended TLS" which seemed like a happy middle-ground. You can have an extension and you can't hurt anything too much.

Part of the documented process including the involvement of the chairs is to match make

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: I feel the FATT's main function is to ask the authors of drafts to be clear about their security claims and arguments.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Thom Wiggers the aim was that it should, whether or not I achieved that is a question for some formal analyst. https://jhoyla.github.io/draft-jhoyla-tls-extended-key-schedule/main/draft-jhoyla-tls-extended-key-schedule.html

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Proofs are somewhat secondary to having these claims and arguments in the first place

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I think that something should go to FATT if anyone thinks it should (author, chair, or random person).

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: "random person" has been used to try to block ML-KEM

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Well, this is a bit of a non-question.  Motherhood and apple pie.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

I feel the FATT's main function is to ask the authors of drafts to be clear about their security claims and arguments.

This is 75% of the value of formal analysis in general IMO.

I think that something should go to FATT if anyone thinks it should (author, chair, or random person).

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: Last Q: Collaboration interactions should be transparent/recorded.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

chat.author: Britta Hale
chat.text: Authors can always request/collaborate on analysis outside of the IETF. The FATT is for cases that lack analysis and to support WG determination if it is needed.

chat.author: Felix Linker
chat.text: +1 @ Ekr, Mike, Chris

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Having the chairs exercise their judgement seems fine. The WG remains in control in that it can progress or block a document based on the FATTs opinion, or because of the absence of an opinion.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

I don't think I agree with you. IIUC FATT is not required to be a subgroup of TLS.

chat.author: Deirdre Connolly
chat.text:
Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

that is not correct

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I mean, if you compare it to CFRG, the analysis from CFRG very clearly isn't part of the IETF.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Deirdre Connolly said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

that is not correct

Well, we have a very loose definition of WG participant, so arguably just by participating in the FATT you are an IETF participant

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: My understanding of the process: someone indicates that a document might need FATT review, chairs decide to ask FATT for review, FATT review the work to determine whether the analysis that has been performed on the work is sufficient, FATT report their findings to the WG, the WG discusses what to do with the document based on that report.

chat.author: Deirdre Connolly
chat.text: Everything that comes out of the FATT is mere input to the TLSWG's existing consensus processes

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: Let's not get sucked into a discussion about procedure. The FATT is just a mechanism for getting an email to the WG with some opinions on some narrow technical questions which require expertise.

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text: What the WG does with that email is up to the WG in every circumstance.

chat.author: Britta Hale
chat.text: TLS WG discussions have sometimes been very contentious. The FATT is a way to get analysis on documents from people with relevant expertise, even if they have a lower threshold for patience with such style of discussions.

chat.author: Felix Linker
chat.text:
Britta Hale said:

TLS WG discussions have sometimes been very contentious. The FATT is a way to get analysis on documents from people with relevant expertise, even if they have a lower threshold for patience with such style of discussions.

chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

I feel the FATT's main function is to ask the authors of drafts to be clear about their security claims and arguments.

I thought the main function of the FATT was to advise the WG with an expert opinion about the level of formal analysis that is accompanying a given draft. I didn't realize that the FATT actually had anything to do with the draft authors directly?

chat.author: Dennis Jackson
chat.text:
Mike Ounsworth said:

Thom Wiggers said:

I feel the FATT's main function is to ask the authors of drafts to be clear about their security claims and arguments.

I thought the main function of the FATT was to advise the WG with an expert opinion the level of formal analysis that is accompanying a given draft. I didn't realize that the FATT actually had anything to do with the draft authors directly?

It doesn't. Thom's only talking about the eventual outcome

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

I don't think I agree with you. IIUC FATT is not required to be a subgroup of TLS.

I may be wrong but to the best of my understanding, FATT is a design team as per https://github.com/tlswg/tls-fatt#the-fatt-panel-and-fatt-point-person. And RFC2418 does not seem to say that design team can be outside of IETF.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Mike Ounsworth indirectly, that's the same: we tell the WG "the security considerations needs clarification"

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

Jonathan Hoyland said:

Martin Thomson said:

I think that something should go to FATT if anyone thinks it should (author, chair, or random person).

That seems like a DOS vector.

I realize that, but FATT can say no.

Can they? Or is FATT's role just to provide input to be considered by the WG?

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: @Muhammad Usama Sardar You may be right, but I think the more important point is that they don't necessarily want to be exposed to the less nice parts of the mailing list, or simply want to focus on other things.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

I mean, if you compare it to CFRG, the analysis from CFRG very clearly isn't part of the IETF.

IRTF is separate from IETF. I don't think this analogy applies.

chat.text: This is why the chairs-proxy was chosen

chat.author: Martin Thomson

chat.text: I see epoch

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Jonathan Hoyland said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

I don't think I agree with you. IIUC FATT is not required to be a subgroup of TLS.

I may be wrong but to the best of my understanding, FATT is a design team as per https://github.com/tlswg/tls-fatt#the-fatt-panel-and-fatt-point-person. And RFC2418 does not seem to say that design team can be outside of IETF.

I think this is just semantics. The only case I can think of where this has a material difference is if a FATT member is an AI. Or, I guess in theory, a person who is banned from the IETF>

chat.author: David Benjamin
chat.text: The one and only time I've ever seen replay protection show up anywhere was when the window was too small. That was mildly annoying for like 30 seconds but, eh, go make it bigger, big deal. :-)

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: Is support making replay protection mandatory, Replay protection is a fundamentation security property. Users thinking they do not need replay protection often shoot themselves in the foot. There is a reason that RFC 9846 spends several pages talking about the security problems with 0 RTT.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar You may be right, but I think the more important point is that they don't necessarily want to be exposed to the less nice parts of the mailing list, or simply want to focus on other things.

I think it would have taken just one week or so. Saying it was a DOS is over the top of my head. Having delayed passing the draft over to FATT has indeed resulted in delays.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Jonathan Hoyland said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Thom Wiggers said:

FATT are not all IETF participants, the process was designed to avoid them getting involved in messes

I think that's not correct. Procedurally, FATT is a subgroup, i.e., they have to be IETF participants. Whether they join the list or actively participate on the list is a different thing.

I don't think I agree with you. IIUC FATT is not required to be a subgroup of TLS.

I may be wrong but to the best of my understanding, FATT is a design team as per https://github.com/tlswg/tls-fatt#the-fatt-panel-and-fatt-point-person. And RFC2418 does not seem to say that design team can be outside of IETF.

I think this is just semantics. The only case I can think of where this has a material difference is if a FATT member is an AI. Or, I guess in theory, a person who is banned from the IETF>

I am not sure what you are saying. RFC2418 Sec. 6.5 seems very clear: "It is often useful, and perhaps inevitable, for a sub-group of a  working group to develop a proposal to solve a particular problem." 
"sub-group" implies to me that FATT is by definition a part of WG, otherwise they cannot be on FATT. Again, being on the list and actively participating is not a requirement, I guess.

chat.author: Deirdre Connolly
chat.text: Which? Part of not DoS'ing the FATT also involves not handing them work that on its face is simple, has existing literature, etc

"sub-group" implies to me that FATT is by definition a part of WG, otherwise they cannot be on FATT. Again, being on the list and actively participating is not a requirement, I guess.

I guess the question is who did the review, a solicited expert or the FATT member themself.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Short answers (but Yaroslav's upcoming slides will do it better)
Why not post-handshake auth? --> 3 reasons: 1) p-h-a is only defined for challenging the client for a second cert, not defined for a server with 2 certs, and 2) p-h-a has no mechanism to block sending of application data until the 2nd auth has happened (and the authentication state of such data is undefined), and 3) security-correct use of p-h-a requires the application (not the TLS library) to implement enforcement correctly.
Why not exported authenticators --> basically 2 & 3 above.

chat.text: Why encrypting with application traffic secret is a problem?

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
@Thom Wiggers From the paper:
During our analysis of the post-handshake
client authentication, it became apparent that the client does not
receive any explicit confirmation that the server has successfully
received the client’s response. Due to the asynchronous nature of
the post-handshake client authentication, the client may keep re-
ceiving data from the server, and will not be able to determine if the
server has received its authentication message. As a consequence,
the client cannot be sure whether the server sent the data under
the assumption that the client is authenticated.
